title: Pier Pressure

jokes.text: Michael Bluth was talking to his son about his latest test. 'A-minus, huh?' 'Proud of me?' 'Very proud... minus.'
jokes.setup: George Michael asks if Michael is proud of his A-minus grade, expecting a simple yes.
jokes.punchline: Michael says 'Very proud... minus.' — withholding full approval with a single beat pause, turning the grade itself into a punchline.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy — Michael repurposes the grammatical structure of 'A-minus' to deliver his disapproval with a single word. The pause before 'minus' is everything. It's character-specific (Michael is a loving but high-achieving pressure parent), structurally elegant, and highly quotable. One of the episode's strongest cold opens.

jokes.text: George Michael's grade card reads 'C-minus' and the narrator spells it out: 'C' M-I-N-E-S
jokes.setup: George Michael's test grade is shown as 'C-minus.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator phonetically spells it as 'C M-I-N-E-S' — the grade is literally 'sea mines,' a layered visual/wordplay gag.
jokes.explanation: Classic AD density — the narrator's phonetic spelling turns a grade into 'sea mines,' which is a throwaway gag that rewards attentive viewers. Not huge in impact but exemplifies the show's layered approach. Rewatch bonus because viewers often miss the wordplay on first pass.

jokes.text: 'Did you even study?' 'No, I didn't.' 'Wow. How impressive is that?'
jokes.setup: Maeby admits she didn't study and got a C-minus, expecting a lecture.
jokes.punchline: Michael responds with heavy sarcasm — 'Wow. How impressive is that?' — cutting but controlled.
jokes.explanation: Functional sarcasm from Michael, competent but not exceptional. Sets up the parenting theme without being particularly original. Gets the scene moving but doesn't stand on its own.

jokes.text: Lindsay defends Maeby's C-plus; Michael corrects 'C-plus' to 'C-minus.' This mirrors the A-minus correction from the opening.
jokes.setup: Lindsay says 'You think she's gonna suddenly turn to drugs 'cause she got a C-plus?'
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects 'Minus.' — echoing his earlier correction of George Michael's grade, establishing this as a running motif.
jokes.explanation: The show efficiently establishes a running gag in under a minute. The 'minus' correction is now a pattern, and Lindsay inflating the grade upward (from C-minus to C-plus) tells us everything about her permissive parenting style. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'You're as bad as Dad with his ridiculous lessons.' — followed immediately by a flashback of George Sr. staging an elaborate J. Walter Weatherman scenario.
jokes.setup: Michael tells Lindsay she's as bad as their father with his 'ridiculous lessons.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately cuts to an insanely elaborate lesson involving a one-armed man, fake blood, and staged trauma — completely validating and then wildly exceeding what 'ridiculous' means.
jokes.explanation: The structural joke is the juxtaposition: Michael's dismissive label 'ridiculous lessons' is immediately followed by something so far beyond ridiculous it recontextualizes the word entirely. The escalation from mild complaint to full elaborate trauma-staging is a classic AD move. High rewatch value because the entire episode's plot hinges on this setup.

jokes.text: J. Walter Weatherman flashback: 'Why are you hitting yourself? Why are you hitting yourself?' / 'We're out of milk.' / 'I could have got it earlier if someone would have left a note.'
jokes.setup: A staged scenario where George Sr. is pretending to be hit by a one-armed man to teach kids to leave notes.
jokes.punchline: The detail that the lesson is about something as mundane as leaving a note about milk makes the elaborate trauma theater completely disproportionate and absurd.
jokes.explanation: The comedic premise is exceptional: a father so committed to teaching mundane lessons that he employs a one-armed man to fake a traumatic accident. The punchline — it was about leaving a note about milk — is brilliantly disproportionate. 'Why are you hitting yourself?' recontextualizes school-bully taunting into a surreal adult trauma. High dark comedy value.

jokes.text: J. Walter Weatherman's arm 'falls off' with fake blood, and George Sr. delivers: 'And that's why you always leave a note.'
jokes.setup: The elaborate fake-arm trauma reaches its peak — blood everywhere, extreme staging.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. calmly and sincerely delivers 'And that's why you always leave a note' — the lesson is horrifyingly banal for what just happened.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's marquee joke and one of the great running gags in sitcom history. The structure is perfect: elaborate absurdist horror → mundane moral lesson. 'And that's why you always leave a note' becomes a cultural touchstone, repeated throughout the series. George Sr.'s complete sincerity is what sells it. Exceptional character integration — only this family, only this father. Comparable to the 'Snip, snap' anchor in cultural impact within the show's universe.

jokes.text: 'Those lessons worked, didn't they? We still leave notes to this day.' / 'Oh, that's what that was about. I thought he was trying to get us off dairy.'
jokes.setup: Michael defends their father's insane lesson by noting they still leave notes.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay reveals she completely missed the lesson's point and thought the milk detail meant George Sr. was anti-dairy — perfectly capturing her obliviousness.
jokes.explanation: The callback punchline that completes the J. Walter Weatherman intro sequence. Lindsay's interpretation — that a traumatic arm-loss staging was actually a message about dairy — is perfect character comedy. It tells us everything about her capacity for self-delusion and missing the obvious. The joke is earned because it flows naturally from both characters' established personalities. Excellent.

jokes.text: Tobias arrives asking for a favor from Michael. Michael's response: 'What happened to, "Hello. I need a favor"?'
jokes.setup: Tobias barges in and immediately says 'I owe Hot Cops 500 bucks.'
jokes.punchline: Michael's complaint isn't about the favor, it's about Tobias skipping the greeting — treating the absurd as if basic etiquette is the real issue.
jokes.explanation: Solid sitcom joke — Michael's response is funny because it treats the etiquette violation as more offensive than the bizarre content of the request. Good character work for Michael as the straight man who insists on normalcy, but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Tobias reveals he used to work for 'Hot Cops,' a stripping agency, while wearing a cop uniform.
jokes.setup: Tobias arrives in a cop uniform, which initially reads as a normal costume or prank.
jokes.punchline: He casually reveals he worked for a male stripper agency called 'Hot Cops' — the uniform is his former work clothes.
jokes.explanation: Peak Tobias — the revelation that he was a male stripper (in a cop uniform, no less) is perfectly in character for this oblivious, sexually confused man. The show mines this for the entire episode. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'I might have been just a little too believable.' — followed by flashback of police pounding on a door and a woman screaming.
jokes.setup: Tobias explains why Hot Cops is upset with him — he 'never delivered on the gig.'
jokes.punchline: He thinks the problem is that he was 'too believable' as a cop — implying he actually scared the clients rather than stripping. The flashback of terrified screaming confirms this.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's complete lack of awareness about why being a terrifyingly convincing cop is bad at a strip-o-gram is solid character comedy. His delivery of 'just a little too believable' with pride makes it land.

jokes.text: Flashback: 'Police! Open up!' [dramatic door-breaking] — implying Gob actually raided a home while working as a stripper
jokes.setup: Gob says he 'might have been just a little too believable' in his cop costume
jokes.punchline: Flashback shows Gob in full cop-mode, apparently conducting a real police raid
jokes.explanation: Visual payoff that extends the joke beyond the dialogue. Gob performing an actual door-breach as a stripper cop is absurdist gold. Works because it's shown, not told, and is 100% consistent with Gob's character.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'm gonna give you the cash, but, in return, I get to ask you for a favor sometime.' Tobias: 'My gut is telling me "No," but my gut is also very hungry.'
jokes.setup: Michael offers a transactional deal — money for a future favor.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's gut instinct says no, but he immediately undermines his own metaphor by noting his gut is literally hungry — collapsing abstract wisdom into physical appetite.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, economical joke. Tobias literalizes a figurative expression in a way that's very on-brand for his character — he's earnest and dim, and the self-undermining move is well-constructed. High quotability.

jokes.text: 'I'm not an A.T.M.' 'I keep forgetting how hilarious you are.'
jokes.setup: Michael refuses to give Gob money by saying he's not an ATM
jokes.punchline: Gob's deadpan sarcasm — 'I keep forgetting how hilarious you are' — positions Michael's cliché refusal as if it were a lame joke
jokes.explanation: Functional sibling banter. Gob reframing Michael's straight-man line as an unfunny joke is a nice meta-beat. Works but doesn't transcend.

jokes.text: Lucille greets Michael and says she needs a favor. Michael: 'We ought to put that on our family crest.'
jokes.setup: Three consecutive characters have now asked Michael for a favor in the same episode.
jokes.punchline: Michael's meta-observation that the family's defining trait is asking him for favors — suggesting it should literally be their heraldic motto.
jokes.explanation: Good observational joke that functions as a meta-comment on the episode's structure. Michael is both the punchline and the straight man — the family constantly exploits his competence. The 'family crest' image is an elegant, slightly aristocratic way to frame their dysfunction.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'They want me to prove no new purchases have been made with company money.' Michael: 'And there haven't been, right?' Lucille: 'Not after we doctor all these receipts.'
jokes.setup: Michael hopefully asks if his mother has been honest with the SEC.
jokes.punchline: Lucille responds not with a denial but with a practical detail about evidence-tampering — she doesn't even register that this is wrong.
jokes.explanation: Classic Lucille. The joke is her total moral casualness — she doesn't admit guilt, she just explains the workaround, as if doctoring receipts is an ordinary household task. Perfect character integration for a woman who has never considered ethical constraints.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I've got a job. I gotta make some money so you can buy more things and destroy the evidence.'
jokes.setup: Michael declines to help Lucille cover up fraud.
jokes.punchline: His sarcastic summary — that his job is to fund her crime — is a perfect deadpan distillation of the family dynamic.
jokes.explanation: Solid Michael deadpan. The efficiency is great — two clauses, complete picture of the Bluth dysfunction. Not a showstopper but very functional and character-true.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Buster's girlfriend, Lucille Austero, suffers from chronic vertigo... which she has been trying to fight without the use of medication.'
jokes.setup: Buster mentions his girlfriend 'is down again.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator explains she has vertigo and is refusing medication — the phrase 'down again' is literal (she's fallen over), and the image of her stubbornly fighting dizziness without help is quietly absurd.
jokes.explanation: AD's narrator earns laughs through clinical specificity. The joke is in the contrast: Buster's emotional 'she's down again' vs. the narrator's matter-of-fact medical exposition. Rewatch bonus for the layered double meaning of 'down.'

jokes.text: Buster: 'Her nausea's gotten really bad. It's really been difficult on us.' / 'I did refer to it as our nausea. But you know, that's when we were going at it really hot and heavy.'
jokes.setup: Buster says the nausea has been 'difficult on us.'
jokes.punchline: He explains the 'our nausea' phrasing was from when they were 'going at it hot and heavy' — the image of Buster and Lucille 2 in a passionate phase is inherently cringe-inducing given the dynamics.
jokes.explanation: Buster-specific cringe — his over-identification with his girlfriend's symptoms by calling it 'our nausea' is a wonderful character detail showing his codependency. The 'hot and heavy' qualifier makes it worse in the best possible way.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Well, now it's my nausea.' — correcting himself after slipping back into 'our.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells Buster 'you're free to go — it's not like you made a commitment.' Buster starts to agree, then catches himself.
jokes.punchline: Buster's self-correction from 'our nausea' to 'my nausea' — then correcting again back — shows he's so enmeshed he can't even articulate the distinction.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the 'our nausea' joke. Buster's real-time struggle with the pronoun reflects his arrested development (no pun intended) — he can't draw a line between himself and the people he attaches to. Character-specific and efficient.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Maybe I don't feel like being criticized around-the-clock.' Lucille: 'I don't criticize you. And if you're worried about criticism... sometimes a diet is the best defense.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay says she doesn't want to be criticized by Lucille.
jokes.punchline: Lucille immediately offers unsolicited diet advice in response to the complaint about criticism — she has zero self-awareness that this IS criticism.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Lucille moments. The ironic structure is perfect: she denies being critical, then is immediately critical. The specific pivot to diet advice is inspired — it's exactly what Lucille would say, it's cutting, and the lack of self-awareness is total. 'Sometimes a diet is the best defense' is a standalone great line. High quotability.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Either way, it's above a "D," right?' Lucille: 'Another world-beater.'
jokes.setup: Maeby tries to spin a D-minus as practically a D.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's withering 'Another world-beater' — dripping with contempt while technically being a reaction, not a direct insult.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's sarcasm is perfectly calibrated — maximum withering in two words. 'World-beater' is a great word choice because it sounds like a compliment until you hear her tone. Very economical, very Lucille.

jokes.text: Maeby asks if 'Lindsay' is spelled with an 'A' or an 'E' — while signing her own mother's name to a report card.
jokes.setup: Lindsay refuses to sign Maeby's bad report card, so Maeby says she'll sign it herself.
jokes.punchline: Maeby doesn't even know how to spell her own mother's name — which simultaneously shows her audacity, her independence, and her genuine ignorance of basic family information.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke. Maeby's brazenness in forging her mother's signature is funny enough, but not knowing how to spell her own mother's name is the kicker that makes it special. It tells us about their disconnected relationship, Maeby's negligent upbringing, and her total confidence while being completely incompetent. Well-constructed, character-specific.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Okay, I know you got a "crocodile" in spelling, but this has gone too far.'
jokes.setup: Maeby can't spell her mother's name.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay references Maeby getting a 'crocodile' in spelling — meaning the progressive school gave animal names instead of grades, and Maeby's best spelling grade was apparently 'crocodile' (implying bottom tier).
jokes.explanation: Wonderful throwaway worldbuilding joke — the progressive school with animal-based grades, and 'crocodile' as Maeby's spelling achievement. The listener has to infer the grading hierarchy (presumably crocodile = bad but not the worst). Classic AD density — it adds texture to Lindsay's negligent parenting choices while being funny in its specificity.

jokes.text: Lindsay attempts to punish Maeby: 'You are now punished. I punish thee.' / 'Are you serious?' / 'What could you possibly come up with that would punish me?' / 'Oh, I have to come up with another thing?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay declares Maeby is punished, using archaic and theatrical language ('I punish thee').
jokes.punchline: When Maeby challenges her to name an actual punishment, Lindsay deflates completely: 'Oh, I have to come up with another thing?' — revealing she had no plan whatsoever.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's parenting incompetence at its finest. 'I punish thee' is a great line on its own — theatrical overcompensation from someone who has no idea what she's doing. But the perfect punchline is 'Oh, I have to come up with another thing?' — revealing that declaring punishment and implementing punishment are two completely separate problems she never connected. Excellent structure and character integration. High quotability.

jokes.text: Lindsay punishes Maeby by sending her to work for Lucille. Maeby reacts with extreme horror ('No! No!' screaming), then Lindsay smiles having found her punishment.
jokes.setup: Lindsay was unable to come up with a punishment. She then remembers what was just painful to her.
jokes.punchline: Sending Maeby to work for Lucille — the same thing that is clearly torture to Lindsay — is presented as a triumphant parenting breakthrough, with Maeby's reaction confirming it's terrible.
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the punishment setup — Lindsay's solution is to transfer her own suffering to Maeby. The irony that this is actually an effective punishment (Maeby's horror confirms it) makes it funnier. Works as a structural callback.

jokes.text: Lindsay's punishment for Maeby: working for Gangy (Lucille). Maeby's horrified reaction.
jokes.setup: Lindsay, inspired by Lucille's brief visit, devises a punishment for Maeby
jokes.punchline: The punishment is spending the day with Lucille — the most feared figure in the family — and Maeby's reaction ('No. No, that's not the answer') confirms its effectiveness
jokes.explanation: Functional but not exceptional. The idea that Lucille is a more effective punishment than anything Lindsay could devise is good character comedy, but the execution is somewhat predictable once the setup is laid.

jokes.text: Michael notices George Michael's eyes are red and worries he's on drugs — but the actual reason is stress from over-studying.
jokes.setup: Michael sees George Michael's red eyes and interprets it as drug use.
jokes.punchline: The irony is that George Michael's stress-induced symptoms look like drug symptoms precisely because Michael pushed him so hard with academics — Michael's parenting caused the evidence he's now misreading.
jokes.explanation: Structurally clever — the episode's central misunderstanding (Michael thinking George Michael is on drugs) is built on an irony that only the audience fully appreciates. Michael's own pressure created the red-eyed, stressed kid he now suspects of drug use. Good dramatic irony that rewards the careful viewer.

jokes.text: Michael tells George Michael to close his textbook and relax. George Michael slowly, agonizingly, cannot bring himself to remove his finger from the book. Michael has to coach him beat by beat.
jokes.setup: Michael tells a stressed George Michael to shut the book and take the night off.
jokes.punchline: George Michael cannot physically close the book — his anxiety and work ethic are so deeply ingrained that the simple act of stopping studying requires Michael's sustained coaching. Multiple beats of 'Your finger is still in the book' extend the joke.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully sustained physical comedy bit. George Michael's inability to close the book is both funny and genuinely touching as character work — he's so conditioned by Michael's pressure that relaxation is physically impossible. 'Your finger is still in the book' is a great recurring beat within the bit. Excellent character integration — only George Michael would struggle this hard with leisure.

jokes.text: Narrator: Buster 'remembered a study he took part in on medical marijuana and its effects on nausea. The subject took the drug in the form of a T.H.C. pill. Nausea was then induced with a day at the carnival... which is what attracted Buster to the experiment in the first place.'
jokes.setup: The narrator explains a legitimate medical marijuana study.
jokes.punchline: The detail that Buster joined a nausea study because he wanted to go to the carnival — the scientific methodology was just a pretext for rides — reveals Buster's childlike reasoning perfectly.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan specificity is what makes this work — the clinical setup of 'nausea was then induced' followed by the completely non-clinical reason Buster signed up. It's the perfect reveal of Buster's eternal childishness through a formally grown-up context.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Buster was found two days later trying to eat his way through this enormous plaster donut.'
jokes.setup: The medical marijuana study backstory continues.
jokes.punchline: Buster's response to the drug: try to eat a decorative plaster donut for two days. The specificity ('this enormous plaster donut') and the matter-of-fact narrator delivery make it exceptional.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's funniest throwaway lines. 'Trying to eat his way through this enormous plaster donut' is absurd, specific, visual, and delivered with complete narrator neutrality. 'This' before 'enormous plaster donut' implies it's a specific, known donut — as if it's a community landmark Buster was attacking. Exceptional economy. Also sets up a visual gag that the audience can picture vividly. Strong rewatch value as a background detail in the world of the show.

jokes.text: George Michael is introduced as 'the toughest, most streetwise kid' Buster knows — then shown at a banana stand in a paper hat.
jokes.setup: Narrator says Buster went to 'the toughest, most streetwise kid he knew' for drug connections.
jokes.punchline: Cut to: George Michael, a nerdy teenager in a banana stand uniform, clearly the least streetwise person in Newport Beach.
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator irony — the buildup to 'toughest, most streetwise' creates an expectation the visual immediately destroys. It's also a character joke (Buster is so sheltered that the banana stand kid IS his most worldly contact). The joke works on setup/punchline and visual gag levels simultaneously.

jokes.text: Buster: 'You work here. This place is pot central, right?' — referring to the Bluth's Original Frozen Banana stand.
jokes.setup: Buster asks George Michael to get marijuana, reasoning that the banana stand must be a drug hub.
jokes.punchline: His logic: the banana stand = 'pot central.' No other reasoning provided. Buster genuinely believes this.
jokes.explanation: Buster's reasoning is perfectly absurd — the banana stand is 'pot central' for reasons only he understands. This is immediately followed by the narrator explaining the historical connection, making Buster accidentally correct in the most roundabout way possible. Rewatch bonus for the payoff.

jokes.text: Narrator explains the banana stand's history as a '70s marijuana meeting spot 'because of the banana's resemblance to a yellow marijuana cigarette.' Then: a jingle — '♪ Big yellow joint, the big yellow joint / I'll meet you down at the big yellow joint ♪'
jokes.setup: The narrator provides historical context for why the banana stand was a drug meeting spot.
jokes.punchline: The banana-as-joint visual logic, then an actual sung jingle about 'the big yellow joint' that is both historically ridiculous and catchy — and will recur throughout the episode.
jokes.explanation: The 'big yellow joint' jingle is genuinely inspired — a fake historical folk song about a banana stand as a drug meetup that is simultaneously absurdist worldbuilding, a visual gag (banana = joint), and a running gag that plays multiple times. The narrator's po-faced historical framing makes it even funnier. High originality, high quotability, strong rewatch value as it returns twice more.

jokes.text: Buster asks George Michael to relay a drug request to Gob 'but you can't tell him it's for me' because 'he always makes fun of me for dating Lucille.' George Michael: 'Everybody does.' Buster: 'That's horrible.'
jokes.setup: Buster explains he doesn't want Gob to know the weed is for him because Gob mocks his relationship with Lucille 2.
jokes.punchline: George Michael casually confirms 'everybody does' mock Buster for this — which Buster finds genuinely shocking, despite it being completely obvious.
jokes.explanation: Nice deadpan from George Michael — his 'everybody does' is delivered as a simple fact, not a joke, which makes it land harder. Buster's wounded 'that's horrible' adds a character beat: he really didn't know. Good economy.

jokes.text: George Michael tells Buster he 'can't tell him it's for you.' Then Michael shows up and scolds George Michael for being at work when he told him to take the day off — then tells him to go be young and hands him $20.
jokes.setup: Michael told George Michael to take a day off and have fun. George Michael came to work anyway.
jokes.punchline: Michael finds him at work and gives him $20 to 'go crazy' — simultaneously rewarding the behavior he's scolding and failing to actually make George Michael rest.
jokes.explanation: Solid but not exceptional. The joke is in the contradiction of Michael's approach to rest ('go crazy' with $20), but it's more plot-functional than comedically sharp.

jokes.text: Buster adds $225 to George Michael's 'go crazy' money to fund the drug buy, immediately launching into '♪ Big yellow joint ♪' again.
jokes.setup: Michael gives George Michael $20 to have fun.
jokes.punchline: Buster immediately adds $225 to fund the marijuana purchase, and the 'big yellow joint' jingle plays again — the drug money being funneled through the banana stand is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Good second use of the jingle — the callback is earned because now the banana stand actually IS a drug money transfer point, making the historical joke literal. Works as a running gag payoff.

jokes.text: Maeby is working with Lucille cataloging valuables. Lucille shows a photo 'from when your Pop-Pop yelled out, "Oh, Melanie..." when he was making love to Gangy.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is showing Maeby family photos as they catalog valuables.
jokes.punchline: One photo is from when George Sr. called out another woman's name during sex with Lucille — which Lucille recounts to her granddaughter with complete casualness.
jokes.explanation: Lucille sharing graphic marital infidelity details with her teenage granddaughter while cataloging valuables is a perfect character moment — she has no filter and no concept of appropriate disclosure. The matter-of-fact delivery ('when he was making love to Gangy') while showing a photograph makes it genuinely uncomfortable in the best way.

jokes.text: Lucille offers Maeby a brooch that Lindsay always wanted, noting 'it's an elephant and I didn't want to invite the comparison.'
jokes.setup: Lucille says Lindsay always wanted this particular brooch.
jokes.punchline: She explains she didn't want to give it to Lindsay because it's an elephant — implying Lindsay is fat. Delivered with complete politeness.
jokes.explanation: One of Lucille's finest passive-aggressive moments. 'I didn't want to invite the comparison' is perfectly constructed — it's mean, it's indirect, it's technically about the jewelry, and it's devastating. The polite framing of a truly cruel thought is quintessential Lucille. High character integration.

jokes.text: Lucille to Maeby, after giving her the brooch: 'Oh, Pretty. It'll distract from the freckles.'
jokes.setup: Lucille gives Maeby the brooch as a gift.
jokes.punchline: Compliment immediately undercut: the brooch is 'pretty' because it will hide Maeby's freckles — another backhanded Lucille compliment targeting appearance.
jokes.explanation: Effective continuation of Lucille's backhanded compliment streak. Less devastating than the elephant line because freckles are a less loaded target than weight, but the rhythm is perfect — gift, compliment, immediate qualification.

jokes.text: George Michael leaves a note on the yacht for Gob because 'as he'd been trained to do since childhood, [he] left a note.'
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives at the yacht to ask Gob about drugs and finds it empty.
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes he left a note 'as he'd been trained to do since childhood' — directly calling back the J. Walter Weatherman lesson. The training worked — just in a context George Sr. could never have anticipated.
jokes.explanation: A masterful callback — the note-leaving lesson from the elaborate J. Walter Weatherman trauma has succeeded, but it's now the mechanism by which George Michael accidentally implicates himself in a drug deal. The lesson worked! Just catastrophically. The narrator's deadpan framing ('as he'd been trained to do since childhood') is the perfect delivery vehicle. High rewatch value because it's the episode's structural spine paying off.

jokes.text: Gob shows Michael the note George Michael left. '$200.' '$100.' '100. I accidentally said 200. I don't know...'
jokes.setup: Gob shows Michael that George Michael left a note and $200 for marijuana.
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects '$200' to '$100.' Gob awkwardly walks back the claim: 'I accidentally said 200. I don't know...' — revealing he was trying to pocket the extra $100.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob moment — caught in a petty con, his backpedal ('I accidentally said 200, I don't know...') is exactly how someone with Gob's confidence and intelligence would handle being exposed. The pause and vague excuse are funnier than any elaborate lie would be. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Gob about the $100 drug money: 'Really, a hundred dollars. You can't get good weed for a hundred bucks.'
jokes.setup: Michael is horrified that George Michael left money for marijuana.
jokes.punchline: Gob's primary concern is not the drug use but the insufficient budget — he's a connoisseur apparently judging the quality that can be procured at this price point.
jokes.explanation: Gob's complete misreading of what matters — not 'my nephew is buying drugs' but 'my nephew can't afford decent drugs' — is perfectly in character. Delivered with the authority of someone who knows the market.

jokes.text: Gob then hands Michael a bag of marijuana: 'Anyway, here you go. Now we are even on you paying off that Hot Cops thing.'
jokes.setup: Gob was owed a favor by Michael for the Hot Cops debt.
jokes.punchline: Gob considers drug delivery to be the equivalent favor — now 'we're even.' The absurd logic of debt settlement via drug procurement is very Gob.
jokes.explanation: Functional plot joke that also reveals Gob's warped sense of fairness and reciprocity. The fact that procuring drugs is how he chooses to even the score says everything about his character.

jokes.text: Michael hears about the A-minus and immediately suspects drug use: 'He's been stressed, his eyes have been red, his grades are dropping. Heard about the A-minus.'
jokes.setup: Michael lists evidence that George Michael is on drugs.
jokes.punchline: The final 'evidence' is an A-minus — he's treating an excellent grade as proof of drug addiction.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the opening. The A-minus that was a disappointment is now evidence of drug use — Michael's parental anxiety has inverted his own standards. Rewatch bonus for catching the structural echo.

jokes.text: Michael confronts George Michael: 'Your Uncle Gob seems to think he saw you down at the docks today. Was that you?' / 'No, no. Maybe it was the other George Michael... the singer-songwriter.' / 'Yeah, that makes sense.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if George Michael was at the docks. George Michael offers the alibi that it was 'the other George Michael — the singer-songwriter.'
jokes.punchline: Michael says 'Yeah, that makes sense' — completely accepting an impossible explanation because he doesn't want to believe his son is lying, and/or because George Michael's earnestness makes even absurd claims feel plausible.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. George Michael's excuse is absurdly bad ('the singer-songwriter'), but Michael's complete acceptance of it reveals either his own denial or his son's innocent demeanor making even terrible lies work. The joke operates on two levels: the implausibility of the excuse AND Michael's willingness to believe it. Both characters are exposed in two lines. Exceptional economy and structure.

jokes.text: Michael: 'What did you do with the money Buster and I gave you?' George Michael: 'I put that in my savings account.' Michael: 'Is that right?' George Michael: 'Yeah. Save up.' Michael: 'Okay. Thanks for being honest.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks what happened to the money; George Michael claims to have saved it.
jokes.punchline: Michael thanks George Michael for 'being honest' — but George Michael is lying. Michael is so eager to believe in his son's goodness that he's thanking him for a lie. The irony is rich.
jokes.explanation: Strong dramatic irony moment — Michael's thanking George Michael for honesty while being lied to is both funny and slightly sad. The simple 'Yeah. Save up.' from George Michael is perfectly delivered earnest lying. Works well as a character beat.

jokes.text: Michael's talking-head: 'He's lying to me. Can you believe that? Where the hell is this family's morality?'
jokes.setup: Michael suspects George Michael is lying about the money.
jokes.punchline: Michael appeals to morality — while he himself is planning to stage a fake drug bust to manipulate his son. The irony of the least self-aware moment from the most self-righteous character.
jokes.explanation: Good irony — Michael's indignation about deception is undercut by his own plotting. Works better on rewatch when you know the full scope of Michael's plan. Strong character integration for Michael as the self-righteous hypocrite.

jokes.text: Michael and Buster discuss whether to yell at George Michael. Buster says: 'Didn't Dad teach us that we don't yell in this family?' — immediately cut to a flashback of George Sr. in a speedboat, unable to hear the warning not to hit the gas.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if he should yell at George Michael; Buster cites a family lesson about not yelling.
jokes.punchline: Flashback: the lesson involved someone on a boat who couldn't hear 'don't hit the gas' over the engine — so the lesson was taught by making the exact mistake. Classic George Sr. escalation.
jokes.explanation: Second George Sr. lesson flashback, brilliantly parallel to the first. The structure is perfect: again, the lesson is taught through over-the-top dangerous staging, and again the lesson is about something mundane. 'That's why you don't yell' delivered with the same calm as 'that's why you always leave a note' is a great structural echo. High rewatch value.

jokes.text: The boat flashback ends: 'That's why you don't yell.' / 'Yeah, yelling's not a good way to go.'
jokes.setup: The elaborate boat disaster lesson about yelling concludes.
jokes.punchline: The exact phrase structure 'That's why you don't yell' echoes 'And that's why you always leave a note' — confirming this as the show's signature lesson-delivery format.
jokes.explanation: The structural echo of the episode's signature line is a mark of excellent writing. Michael's casual acceptance 'yeah, yelling's not a good way to go' while missing that he just watched another insane lesson play out is perfect straight-man comedy. The parallelism is intentional and rewards viewers who caught the first instance.

jokes.text: Michael asks George Sr. (in prison) to help set up a fake drug bust with J. Walter Weatherman. George Sr.: 'He's, uh, dead. You killed him when you left the door open with the air conditioner running.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks how to reach J. Walter Weatherman for the staged drug bust.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. casually informs Michael that J. Walter Weatherman is dead — and that Michael killed him by leaving a door open with the AC running. A lesson within a lesson within a lesson.
jokes.explanation: Stunning dark comedy escalation. The lessons worked so well that Michael following one of them (presumably) killed the man who enabled them. The specific cause of death ('left the door open with the air conditioner running') is both absurdly mundane and perfectly in the show's logic. This is also a false alarm — George Sr. is lying, and J. Walter Weatherman is alive — making it a structural misdirection as well.

jokes.text: Michael explains his plan for a fake drug bust 'down by the docks.' George Sr.: 'Tonight? No. It's Yontif. It's the first night of Yom Kippur.'
jokes.setup: Michael wants to stage the fake drug bust tonight.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. — in prison for massive financial fraud — objects on religious grounds. The incongruity of a man who staged traumatic scenarios for his children suddenly becoming observant is very funny.
jokes.explanation: Good character contradiction joke — George Sr.'s sudden religiosity in prison (where religion offers legitimacy, structure, and possibly exemptions) is absurd given his moral history. The Yom Kippur detail is very specific and very Jewish comedy, which fits the show's sensibility.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Dad, that's just one night, and it's back in September.' George Sr.: 'That's okay. You've only been a Jew about two days.'
jokes.setup: Michael points out that Yom Kippur was months ago.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. concedes Michael's point by noting he's only recently converted — implying he has no actual religious knowledge and is faking his observance. His new Jewish identity is purely performative.
jokes.explanation: Excellent follow-up to the Yom Kippur joke. 'You've only been a Jew about two days' perfectly confirms that George Sr.'s conversion is recent, opportunistic, and shallow. The casual delivery ('that's okay') before the devastating qualifier is well-constructed.

jokes.text: George Sr. checks the time: 'What time is it? Oh, almost sundown. I have to prepare for the Sabbath.' Michael: 'It's Tuesday.' George Sr.: 'Shh, shh, shh.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. suddenly announces he must prepare for the Sabbath.
jokes.punchline: Michael points out it's Tuesday. George Sr.'s response is not correction or explanation but 'Shh, shh, shh' — his standard dismissal noise when caught.
jokes.explanation: Perfect capper to the fake religiosity sequence. 'It's Tuesday' is the straight-man fact, and 'Shh, shh, shh' is George Sr.'s signature non-answer — he uses this dismissal whenever caught in a lie, and it works because it acknowledges nothing while ending the conversation. The economy is exceptional — the whole joke is in that one sound.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'That was supposed to be for me. She was my au pair. I'm the one who cleared my throat and pointed to the laundry room.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay sees Maeby wearing the brooch Lucille gave away.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay explains her claim to the brooch: 'I cleared my throat and pointed to the laundry room' — implying she sent the au pair to have sex with George Sr., and this brooch was her reward/acknowledgment.
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully dark joke that rewards the listener who puts the implication together. Lindsay is indignant about not getting the brooch because she was the one who facilitated her father's affair with the au pair — 'cleared my throat and pointed to the laundry room' is a perfect euphemism for something deeply inappropriate. Her complete confidence in her claim makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Lindsay tries to lure Maeby away from Lucille with ice cream. Lucille: 'That chubby little wrist of yours is testing the tensile strength of this bracelet as it is.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay offers Maeby ice cream to get her to leave Lucille's apartment.
jokes.punchline: Lucille responds to the ice cream offer by noting Maeby's wrist is too fat for her bracelet — redirecting from the invitation to a physical put-down with 'tensile strength' used with engineering precision about a child's wrist.
jokes.explanation: 'Tensile strength' is a wonderfully clinical word for a grandmother to use about her granddaughter's wrist. Lucille's body criticism extending to Maeby (following the earlier freckle comment) shows she's truly indiscriminate. The word choice elevates this above a generic put-down.

jokes.text: Gob's response to being asked to use Hot Cops for a fake drug bust: 'Michael, these guys are real dancers. They've never done any hot policing.'
jokes.setup: Michael wants Gob to use the Hot Cops for a fake police drug bust on his son.
jokes.punchline: Gob defends the Hot Cops' professional integrity — they are dancers, not police impersonators. This implies he has deep pride in the stripping profession and its separation from actual police work.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully absurd. 'These guys are real dancers' spoken with genuine professional pride about male strippers is peak Gob. The implicit dignity he assigns to 'hot policing' vs. 'real dancing' inverts what we'd consider the professional hierarchy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Michael: 'They don't even need to be hot. They just need to show up in those uniforms.'
jokes.setup: Gob says the Hot Cops are 'real dancers' who can't do policing.
jokes.punchline: Michael's dismissal — 'they don't even need to be hot' — reduces the Hot Cops to their uniforms, missing entirely that their attractiveness is their professional identity.
jokes.explanation: Solid companion joke to Gob's 'real dancers' line. Michael's practical reductionism vs. Gob's professional pride is a good character contrast, but this one doesn't quite land as hard as Gob's setup.

jokes.text: Buster on the phone, pacing: 'I am like a caged animal. My old lady's on the floor, man. I am not s-ing around.'
jokes.setup: Buster is anxious about getting marijuana for Lucille 2.
jokes.punchline: Buster's attempt to sound tough and street-credible ('my old lady's on the floor, man. I am NOT s-ing around') combined with his inability to say 'sh*t' outright and his obvious non-toughness is inherently comic.
jokes.explanation: Buster trying to perform toughness while being manifestly Buster is a reliable comic vein. 'I am NOT s-ing around' from a man who clearly is not a threat to anyone is funny because of the character gap. 'My old lady' for Lucille 2 is an additional layer of trying-too-hard.

jokes.text: Michael's planning conversation with Gob: 'I want him scared. I love this kid.' / 'These guys are pros. They're gonna push the tension to the last possible moment before they strip.' / 'They're not going to strip, are they?' / 'I told them not to, but I can't promise that their instincts won't kick in.'
jokes.setup: Michael is planning a fake drug bust and insists no one strips.
jokes.punchline: Gob can't guarantee it because the Hot Cops' stripping 'instincts' might override instructions — implying stripping is a reflex they can't control, like flinching.
jokes.explanation: Gob's 'I can't promise their instincts won't kick in' is a great line — treating male stripping as a Pavlovian response rather than a choice. It's absurd, in-character, and sets up the payoff when the Hot Cops do indeed start stripping despite the plan. Strong foreshadowing of the escalating chaos to come.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I really appreciate you doing this for me. You're making a very miserable person happy.' George Michael: 'She really feels awful, huh?' Buster: 'Oh. Oh, I was talking about me.'
jokes.setup: Buster thanks George Michael for helping him get marijuana to make 'a very miserable person happy.'
jokes.punchline: George Michael assumes Buster means Lucille 2 (the sick girlfriend). Buster clarifies: he meant himself. His own misery from Lucille 2's illness is primary; her suffering is secondary.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best constructed jokes. The misdirection is perfect — 'a very miserable person' reads as the sick girlfriend, making the reveal that it's Buster himself a sharp character punch. It perfectly encapsulates Buster's narcissistic helplessness — he experiences her illness primarily through his own suffering. The pause before 'Oh. Oh, I was talking about me.' is key. Exceptional structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Gob greets George Michael at the yacht: 'You weren't followed, were you?' — full drug-deal mode for a family transaction involving weed and a one-armed man.
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives at the dock to meet Gob.
jokes.punchline: Gob is playing the role of drug dealer with complete commitment — 'you weren't followed, were you?' — in what is essentially a neighborhood banana stand transaction.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke — Gob's movie-villain over-commitment to the drug deal aesthetic is funny but not exceptional. Good Gob character beat but not a standout moment.

jokes.text: Gob: 'All right, kid. Let's deal some drugs.'
jokes.setup: The dock drug deal is about to happen.
jokes.punchline: Gob announces the transaction with the casual enthusiasm of someone starting a board game — 'Let's deal some drugs.' No one who actually deals drugs says this.
jokes.explanation: Deceptively simple and very quotable. 'Let's deal some drugs' is too cheerful, too casual, and completely wrong in register — someone who actually says this has clearly never dealt drugs. Maximum economy (four words) for a strong Gob character beat.

jokes.text: Gob flashes his boat lights to signal the dealer: 'Flashing the lights... so the dealer knows what's goin' down.' George Michael: 'Now we wait.' Gob: 'How long?' George Michael: 'Who knows? An hour. Maybe five.'
jokes.setup: Gob flashes the lights as a drug signal he apparently learned from movies.
jokes.punchline: He then asks George Michael (the naive teenager) how long they wait — and George Michael's guess of 'an hour. Maybe five' is equally uninformed. Two people performing drug deal competence with zero actual knowledge.
jokes.explanation: Both characters are performing drug-deal expertise they don't have — Gob with the light-flashing, George Michael with his vague timeline. The joke is in their mutual incompetence masquerading as cool. Works but isn't exceptional.

jokes.text: The drug 'dealer' arrives immediately after 'who knows, an hour, maybe five' — screaming 'Drug delivery!'
jokes.setup: Gob and George Michael brace for a long wait for the drug dealer.
jokes.punchline: The dealer arrives within seconds, announces himself with 'Drug delivery!' — the complete opposite of how a drug deal works.
jokes.explanation: 'Drug delivery!' is hilariously wrong — both the speed of arrival and the enthusiastic announcement blow the premise of clandestine drug commerce. Good escalation of the 'nobody here knows what they're doing' theme.

jokes.text: The drug dealer (Hot Cop) starts to take his shirt off. Gob: 'Oh, my God.' Michael (from hiding): 'Nice, right?' Gob: 'Okay, you can put your shirt down, Derek. Just give him the stuff.'
jokes.setup: The fake drug deal is supposed to be convincing. The Hot Cop 'dealer' begins removing his shirt.
jokes.punchline: True to Gob's prediction, the Hot Cops' stripping instincts kick in immediately — the drug dealer can't help himself. Michael from hiding actually appreciates the show ('Nice, right?') before Gob has to manage the situation.
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to Gob's earlier warning. The callback is clean — Gob predicted the stripping instinct would override instructions, and it does, immediately. Michael's amused 'Nice, right?' from hiding is an unexpected character beat (he's momentarily distracted from his parenting mission). Strong structural reward.

jokes.text: The fake cop arrives: 'Police officers! Freeze!' / 'What? Oh, no! It's the cops!' / 'And a construction worker.' / 'Let's get this party started.'
jokes.setup: The fake police show up for the staged drug bust.
jokes.punchline: Gob identifies not just police but 'a construction worker' — a Village People reference acknowledging the Hot Cops are dressed as YMCA archetypes. The construction worker then announces 'Let's get this party started' — blowing the cover completely.
jokes.explanation: The Village People costume logic (police + construction worker) is a great detail, and 'Let's get this party started' is the perfect way for a stripper to destroy a serious moment. The escalation from tense drug bust to party announcement is very funny.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Aren't you guys gonna cuff him?' Hot Cop: 'You wanted handcuffs?'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to get the Hot Cops to perform a convincing arrest.
jokes.punchline: The Hot Cop hears 'cuff him' and responds 'You wanted handcuffs?' — as if this is a special order for a kinky performance, not a police procedure. The framing reveals he thinks in terms of acts, not arrests.
jokes.explanation: Good joke on the stripping-vs.-policing confusion. The Hot Cop's reframing of 'handcuffs' as a performance request rather than a police tool is a clean, efficient bit.

jokes.text: 'Aren't you guys gonna cuff him?' 'You wanted handcuffs?'
jokes.setup: Michael expected a convincing police bust — asks why George Michael hasn't been cuffed
jokes.punchline: The Hot Cop interprets 'handcuffs' as a kinky request rather than a police procedure — revealing that cuffing is in their entertainment repertoire but not as law enforcement
jokes.explanation: Good double-meaning joke. 'You wanted handcuffs?' reframes the police equipment as prop erotica. The Hot Cops' professional context bleeds into every interaction.

jokes.text: Michael reveals the bust to George Michael: 'George Michael, I hope you learned your lesson, pal.' / 'Okay.' / [beat] 'Okay, all right. Thank you. Thank you.'
jokes.setup: Michael dramatically reveals the fake bust was a lesson.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's flat 'Okay.' — followed by Michael having to fill the silence with 'Okay, all right. Thank you. Thank you.' The lesson lands with zero emotional impact, requiring Michael to coach himself through the anticlimactic response.
jokes.explanation: Excellent anticlimactic beat. Michael's elaborate lesson deserves a dramatic 'I've learned my lesson' — instead he gets 'Okay.' The extended beat where he then has to thank himself ('Thank you. Thank you.') because no one else is impressed reveals his desperate need for the lesson to work. Both characters exposed in near-silence.

jokes.text: George Michael finally confesses the drugs weren't for him: 'Okay, it was for me. I was gonna smoke the marijuana like a cigarette.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks whose the drugs were for. George Michael refuses to give up Buster, so he takes the fall.
jokes.punchline: His confession is hilariously specific and naive: 'I was gonna smoke the marijuana like a cigarette.' The qualifier 'like a cigarette' suggests he isn't even sure what other ways there might be — this is someone with zero drug experience describing drug use.
jokes.explanation: 'Smoke the marijuana like a cigarette' is a perfect line — George Michael's innocence and naivety are embedded in the specific phrasing. No one who has ever smoked marijuana would say 'like a cigarette.' It's both funny and genuinely sweet — he's covering for Buster with the only drug knowledge he has, which is essentially zero. High character integration, very quotable.

jokes.text: Buster emerges from the shadows and admits the drugs were for him and his sick girlfriend. Michael: 'Why don't you just wait it out? She's gonna be gone soon.'
jokes.setup: Buster admits his girlfriend Lucille 2 is sick and that's why he needed the marijuana.
jokes.punchline: Michael's solution: 'Why don't you just wait it out? She's gonna be gone soon.' — this could mean 'she'll recover' but clearly sounds like he means 'she'll die soon, just wait.' Buster's reaction confirms this was received as cruel.
jokes.explanation: Classic dark Michael moment — the ambiguity of 'she's gonna be gone soon' works perfectly as both 'she'll recover' and 'she'll die.' Michael doesn't clarify, which suggests he may mean the latter. The casual delivery of something potentially terrible is well-executed.

jokes.text: Buster reacts to Michael's 'she's gonna be gone soon' by shouting 'Oh, that's it!' and then hitting Michael — and immediately the J. Walter Weatherman arm comes off.
jokes.setup: George Sr. told Michael J. Walter Weatherman was dead — Michael believes the lesson can't be staged. The dock scene then organically escalates.
jokes.punchline: J. Walter Weatherman appears and his arm comes off exactly as before — George Sr. staged the lesson anyway, using the actual confrontation on the dock as his setup. The whole fake drug bust was inside George Sr.'s lesson.
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural masterpiece. George Sr. told Michael J. Walter Weatherman was dead — a lie designed to make Michael give up on staging his own lesson. Then George Sr. staged his own lesson INSIDE Michael's lesson, with J. Walter Weatherman appearing just as Michael's fake bust turns real. The inception-like nesting of lesson-within-lesson-within-lesson is exceptional craft. The arm coming off at the perfect moment — after Buster hits Michael — is the culmination of the entire episode's thematic argument. This is the joke that defines the episode's genius and should be the benchmark for the highest scores.

jokes.text: 'It is unbelievable that you'd ask my son to score you pot.' [Michael confronting Buster while himself having just run an elaborate fake drug bust using strippers]
jokes.setup: Michael rightfully confronts Buster for involving George Michael in drug procurement
jokes.punchline: Michael's moral outrage is ironic: he has just staged a fake drug bust using professional strippers to teach his son a lesson — his methods are equally inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Good ironic beat — Michael's hypocrisy is implicit and rewards attentive viewers. Not as pointed as some other ironic Michael moments but functional.

jokes.text: Actual drug dealers arrive: 'Hey, what are you guys doin'?' 'We got your signal. You flashed the lights. We got the drugs. You got the money?'
jokes.setup: The fake drug bust is winding down when real drug dealers show up, responding to Gob's earlier light-flashing
jokes.punchline: Gob's theatrical light-flashing earlier actually summoned real drug dealers — the fake operation attracted genuine criminal enterprise
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation — the fake drug deal has attracted real criminals. The light-flashing callback pays off with genuine stakes. The absurdity of the fake operation being functionally identical to a real one is well-constructed. This is the episode's comic crisis point.

jokes.text: J. Walter Weatherman's arm comes off on the dock: 'Oh, my God! My arm! Oh, my God! This guy's arm just came off and landed on the dock.'
jokes.setup: The moment of the arm detaching on the dock.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's flat description — 'just came off and landed on the dock' — deadpan-narrates what is visually grotesque. The arm coming off is the episode's signature sight gag repeated.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's clinical description of the arm coming off ('just came off and landed on the dock') is the perfect deadpan wrapper for a physical gag. On rewatch, the arm coming off is both a callback payoff and a revelation — you now understand the entire episode was building to this.

jokes.text: Michael confronts George Sr.: 'So you taught me a lesson not to teach lessons?' George Sr.: 'It was my last lesson.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just had the meta-lesson revealed to him.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s 'It was my last lesson' — a promise he will obviously not keep, and which is itself probably a lesson. The circularity is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The coda to the meta-lesson. 'It was my last lesson' has a beautiful irony — it's almost certainly itself a lesson, and George Sr. surely knows this. The absolute confidence of the delivery vs. the obvious impossibility of the promise is great character comedy. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: Maeby returns home and tells Lindsay: 'I'm glad you're my mom.' Lindsay is moved. Maeby then gives Lindsay the brooch 'You deserve it. I can't believe you had to put up with her your whole life.' Lindsay: 'Honey, this is so sweet.' / 'Let's celebrate and go shopping.'
jokes.setup: Maeby has a genuine bonding moment with Lindsay, gives her the brooch, and expresses sympathy.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's response to this touching moment of connection is 'Let's celebrate and go shopping' — she deflects genuine emotion into commerce immediately.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's inability to sustain emotional moments — pivoting immediately to shopping — is good character work but not the sharpest delivery of this joke type the show has done. Works as a character beat.

jokes.text: Maeby reveals she knew the Hot Cops bust was staged: 'One of the Hot Cops is my choir teacher.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is admiring what she thinks was a real (if odd) situation.
jokes.punchline: Maeby knew the whole time because her choir teacher moonlights as a Hot Cop — a perfect coincidence that collapses the distinction between the characters' worlds and reveals Maeby is always one step ahead.
jokes.explanation: Great late-episode reveal — Maeby's choir teacher being a Hot Cop is simultaneously absurd worldbuilding and a character statement about how un-foolable Maeby is. The specific detail (choir teacher) makes it funnier than any generic connection would be. Rewards rewatch because you now wonder about the choir teacher throughout earlier Maeby scenes.

jokes.text: Michael to George Michael: 'I should learn to talk with you a little bit more, huh?' 'I think we're okay.' 'We are, aren't we?' 'You know there's nothing you can't be totally honest with me about. You can say anything to me, anything at all.' George Michael: 'Like, say, I had a crush on my own cousin.'
jokes.setup: Michael delivers a heartfelt speech about open communication and honesty — 'you can say anything to me, anything at all.'
jokes.punchline: George Michael immediately tests this with 'Like, say, I had a crush on my own cousin' — the one thing Michael absolutely cannot handle hearing, delivered with total guilelessness.
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect episode closer. Michael invites total honesty; George Michael immediately uses it to reveal his crush on his cousin Maeby — the exact content that will never be discussed normally, delivered earnestly in response to a direct invitation. The irony that Michael's plea for openness immediately produced the worst possible revelation is exceptional. It's also a season-long thread (George Michael/Maeby) paid off perfectly in a small beat. High character integration — this is exactly what George Michael would do. Among the episode's top 3 jokes.

jokes.text: After George Michael's cousin confession, Michael just says: 'Hey.' — and the scene ends.
jokes.setup: George Michael confesses to having a crush on his cousin Maeby.
jokes.punchline: Michael's entire response is 'Hey.' — not a word of processing, not a question, not a reaction. Complete shutdown. The lesson-delivering, emotionally articulate father is reduced to one syllable.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — Michael's devastation, denial, and shutdown expressed in one word. 'Hey.' as the sum total of a father's response to 'I have a crush on my cousin' is perfect deadpan. The silence that follows does all the work. This is the comedic inverse of Michael's elaborate lesson-planning — confronted with something real, he can only say 'Hey.' Exceptional structure and economy.

jokes.text: George Michael tells Michael: 'You just taught me a lesson.' Michael: 'Oh.'
jokes.setup: Michael's horrified non-reaction to the cousin confession inadvertently taught George Michael something.
jokes.punchline: 'You just taught me a lesson.' — the 'lesson' structure has now escaped George Sr. and Michael and entered George Michael's vocabulary. The whole episode's theme (lessons, their inevitability) lands in a perfect meta-comment, with Michael's devastated 'Oh.' as the final beat.
jokes.explanation: The true final joke of the episode and a structural masterpiece. George Michael naming Michael's involuntary lesson-teaching as 'a lesson' — while the Big Yellow Joint jingle plays — confirms that lessons are unavoidable in the Bluth family, regardless of intent. It's the thematic thesis of the episode delivered as a punchline. Michael's 'Oh.' mirrors his similar shutdown in 'Hey.' and completes the episode's comedic architecture. This, combined with the immediate jingle callback, is the ideal episode closer.

jokes.text: '♪ Big yellow joint, the big yellow joint / I'll meet you down at the big yellow joint ♪' — plays over the final scene as Michael and George Michael stand awkwardly.
jokes.setup: The episode ends with the cousin confession still hanging in the air.
jokes.punchline: The big yellow joint jingle plays one final time — tying the episode's drug subplot, the banana stand, and the uncomfortable final moment together in one absurd musical button.
jokes.explanation: The perfect episode button — the jingle returns for its third and final use as both a thematic tie (the drug subplot resolution) and a tonal release valve after the emotionally charged cousin confession moment. Great structural use of a running gag as an episode-closing device.

tmdb_overview: Buster realizes that medical marijuana will help cure Lucille 2's vertigo, and pays George Michael to get it for him from Gob. Lindsay sends Maeby to spend a day with Lucille to punish her for bad grades. Michael thinks George Michael is on drugs and attempts to teach him a lesson.
insight: Methodical character comedy builds to dark turn—67 jokes in 24 minutes sustains high velocity.
